title: Jonathan Van Maren and Josh Brahm. Yikes.
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman responds point-by-point to a 56-minute Josh Brahm podcast featuring Jonathan Van Maren that attacked the Abolitionist Movement and Russell Hunter, refuting eleven specific arguments about history, immediatism, severability, nullification, and the moral logic of incremental pro-life legislation.
audiences: Abolitionists wanting rebuttals to common pro-life critiques
Pro-life Christians evaluating Van Maren and Brahm's arguments
Historians of slavery abolitionism (Wilberforce, Clarkson, Garrison)
State legislators considering abolition bills with severability/inseverability clauses
Pastors and teachers wrestling with the law-as-tutor question

questions.question: Why does Silberman think Van Maren's podcast attack on Russell Hunter may have been timed strategically?
questions.audience: Abolitionists wanting rebuttals to common pro-life critiques
questions.answer: Silberman speculates the assault on Hunter's character coincides with Free the States exposing the pro-life myth of an incrementalist Wilberforce, just as Hunter is preparing a three-part video series to bust that myth. Van Maren is a primary historian for the Pro-Life Movement and had been silent on abolitionism for six years before swinging at Hunter precisely as that historiographical project nears release.
questions.quote: Van Maren is one of the primary historians for the Pro-Life Movement. It's been near complete radio silence on abolitionism from Van Maren for six years, and now he comes back swinging the sledgehammer against Russell in particular just as Russell is busting the Pro-Life Establishment's favorite historical myth.

questions.question: How do leading historians of American slavery abolitionism define 'abolitionist'?
questions.audience: Historians of slavery abolitionism (Wilberforce, Clarkson, Garrison)
questions.answer: From the 1830s onward, scholars treat 'abolitionist' and 'immediatist' as effectively synonymous in the American context. Pulitzer Prize-winning historian James McPherson defines an abolitionist as one who agitated before the Civil War for the immediate, unconditional, and total abolition of slavery, and History.com gives essentially the same definition.
questions.quote: Pulitzer prize-winning civil war historian James McPherson defines abolitionist "as one who before the Civil War had agitated for the immediate, unconditional, and total abolition of slavery in the United States."

questions.question: Do abortion abolitionists deny that gradual abolitionists existed in British history?
questions.audience: Historians of slavery abolitionism (Wilberforce, Clarkson, Garrison)
questions.answer: No. Silberman explicitly concedes that England had a substantial group of gradualists and that Wilberforce contrasted himself as an 'immediate abolitionist' against 'gradual abolitionists' like Henry Dundas and William Dolben. Modern abolitionists work to maintain the immediatist/incrementalist linguistic distinction not by denying that history but to keep two ideologies separate.
questions.quote: Back then, the linguistic distinction among British anti-slavery factions was between the immediate abolitionists of the slave trade (ie. Wilberforce and Thomas Clarkson) and the gradual abolitionists (ie. Henry Dundas and William Dolben).

questions.question: Are abolitionists being inconsistent when they cite Wilberforce historically and also appeal to Scripture?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christians evaluating Van Maren and Brahm's arguments
questions.answer: No. Silberman argues these are two complementary claims, not a bait-and-switch: Wilberforce was historically an immediatist whose writings instruct us, AND God's word is the ultimate standard that prohibits regulating murder. Holding both arguments simultaneously is not bad faith; it is layered argumentation.
questions.quote: There is nothing dishonest about making both of these arguments simultaneously, yet Brahm and Van Maren accuse abolitionists of being dishonest, bad faith actors for doing so.

questions.question: Is immediatism the same thing as expecting abortion to end overnight?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christians evaluating Van Maren and Brahm's arguments
questions.answer: No. Silberman calls this a strawman. Immediatism concerns what the demand should be, not how long the fight will take. Even Garrison wrote in The Liberator that immediate abolition urged earnestly will 'alas! be gradual abolition in the end' through what Free the States calls the 'steel ball effect' on culture and lawmakers.
questions.quote: The immediatism-incrementalism debate does not revolve around how long the battle takes (though the immediatist strategy will certainly achieve the desired end more quickly). The debate revolves around what our demand should be.

questions.question: Could abortion realistically be abolished quickly in any U.S. state?
questions.audience: State legislators considering abolition bills with severability/inseverability clauses
questions.answer: Silberman argues yes in states like Oklahoma and Missouri, where pro-lifers dominate the population, legislature, and statewide offices. The barrier is not impossibility but the 'delusive scheme of gradualism in submission to the supreme court' gripping Christian politicians who could otherwise suspend the rules and pass an abolition bill.
questions.quote: If the delusive scheme of gradualism in submission to the supreme court lost its grip on the minds of Oklahoma Christians, abortion could be abolished before the legislative session is over.

questions.question: How does Silberman defend Russell Hunter's imprecatory prayer video?
questions.audience: Abolitionists wanting rebuttals to common pro-life critiques
questions.answer: The video responded to Christian leaders branding the entire Abolitionist Movement 'terrorists' and 'jihadis' over the pre-conversion actions of two men who later became Christians, pacifists, and abolitionists. Russell asked God to vindicate whichever side was wrong. Silberman says wielding pre-regeneration sins against born-again believers denies the power of the gospel.
questions.quote: People who would use their actions before they were born again to slander the whole Abolitionist Movement as terrorists were acting wickedly and they were denying the power of the gospel to renew men's souls and minds.

questions.question: Wouldn't a court strip out the abolition portion of an abolition bill while leaving the repeal of pro-life regulations standing?
questions.audience: State legislators considering abolition bills with severability/inseverability clauses
questions.answer: Abolition bills include explicit inseverability clauses that bind the courts. Silberman cannot find a single Oklahoma case of a court ignoring such a clause. Even if a court tried, the same legislative posture that passed an abolition bill would have already shifted power back from the courts and would disregard the ruling.
questions.quote: "The provisions, words, phrases and clauses of this act are declared to be inseverable."

questions.question: How does Free the States respond when pro-life lobbyists keep generating new excuses to oppose abolition bills?
questions.audience: State legislators considering abolition bills with severability/inseverability clauses
questions.answer: They strip the excuse from the bill year after year without watering it down. In 2019 it was nullification; they showed abolitionists were the original nullifiers. In 2020 it was secession; they added a clause disclaiming withdrawal from the Union. In 2021 it became severability; they will adjust the language and the goalposts will move again.
questions.quote: They jump from excuse-to-excuse every year as a way of delaying the abolition of abortion. That'll be gone next year, and the pro-life lobbyists and politicians will be touting their new reason why we can't abolish abortion.

questions.question: Is it true that pro-lifers like Van Maren are helping keep abortion legal?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christians evaluating Van Maren and Brahm's arguments
questions.answer: Silberman says yes, and points to Wilberforce's own warning to gradual abolitionists who could not foresee the practical effect of their opposition. By echoing the lobbyist excuses against abolition bills in eight states, Van Maren is functionally aiding the politicians blocking abolition.
questions.quote: Van Maren is helping keep abortion legal in the eight states an abolition bill has been put forward by parroting the excuses of the very people keeping abortion legal.

questions.question: Why does Silberman call the 'anarchy' charge against state-level abolition bills idolatrous?
questions.audience: Pastors and teachers wrestling with the law-as-tutor question
questions.answer: Because it presupposes that SCOTUS has unconditional authority — there is no immorality the Court could commit that would justify defying it. That posture treats the Court as supreme over God and the Constitution. Silberman frames upholding the right to life as the most basic duty of a state official acting as God's deacon of justice.
questions.quote: Their view presupposes that there is no authority above the supreme court. SCOTUS' power is unconditional. The justices are entirely unconstrained by any law of God or man.

questions.question: What does the 'flag bill' actually demonstrate about Wilberforce's strategy?
questions.audience: Historians of slavery abolitionism (Wilberforce, Clarkson, Garrison)
questions.answer: It was not an incremental compromise but a wartime measure designed to disarm the slave lobby's economic and geopolitical arguments — analogous to a modern state-sovereignty bill like Arkansas SB298 that strips federal-overreach excuses without implying that murdering some children is acceptable. Such bills speed abolition rather than delay it.
questions.quote: The flag bill was not an incremental bill in the sense that pro-life incrementalists use that word. It was a war measure put forward with the intention of taking away economic and geopolitical arguments that the slave lobby was making against abolition.

questions.question: What did Wilberforce himself say about incrementalists like the supporters of the Dolben Act?
questions.audience: Historians of slavery abolitionism (Wilberforce, Clarkson, Garrison)
questions.answer: In A Letter on the Abolition of the Slave Trade, Wilberforce wrote that gradualists who substituted gradual for immediate abolition 'dashed the cup of happiness from the lips of the wretched African' and were the most efficient supporters of the slave trade. He treats incremental half-measures with at least as much contempt as modern abortion abolitionists do.
questions.quote: [T]he gradual Abolitionists have been, in fact, the only real stay of that system of wickedness and cruelty which we wish to abolish; though that assertion is unquestionably true; but it is trying beyond expression that they should be the real maintainers of the Slave Trade

questions.question: Did Wilberforce remain a supporter of the colonization scheme that deported freed slaves to Africa?
questions.audience: Historians of slavery abolitionism (Wilberforce, Clarkson, Garrison)
questions.answer: No. Wilberforce publicly repented after deliberation among British abolitionists informed by Garrison. James Cropper announced to the gathering that Wilberforce 'now deeply regrets that he was ever led to say anything in approbation of the Colonization Society.' Silberman says defining a man by views he renounced is bad historiography.
questions.quote: "It is with very great pleasure that I can add the name of William Wilberforce as having changed his opinion. He now deeply regrets that he was ever led to say anything in approbation of the Colonization Society."

questions.question: What was Clarkson's actual disagreement with Garrison, according to Silberman?
questions.audience: Historians of slavery abolitionism (Wilberforce, Clarkson, Garrison)
questions.answer: Not immediatism — both held it firmly — but Sabbatarianism. Clarkson worried Garrison's rejection of strict Sabbath observance would erode Christian moral principle, while Garrison saw Sunday-only piety as enabling weekday cruelty. Van Maren's own writing on Clarkson's letter at CatholicCitizens.com nowhere claims the dispute was about immediatism.
questions.quote: In reality, Clarkson's concerns with Garrison had nothing to do with immediatism. (Clarkson, like Wilberforce, detailed his fierce opposition to incremental legislation like the Dolben Act in his writings…) Clarkson's concerns were about Garrison's rejection of Sabbatarianism.

questions.question: Why does Silberman insist that even implicitly unjust laws teach injustice?
questions.audience: Pastors and teachers wrestling with the law-as-tutor question
questions.answer: Because law is inherently a tutor. A 20-week ban grounded in pain capability teaches that pain capability gives humans value; a heartbeat bill produces legislators who claim 'life begins at a heartbeat'; rape exceptions tell rape-conceived people their lives are less valuable. Implicit allowances dehumanize preborn children just as explicit ones do.
questions.quote: Laws teach. It is inherent to their nature. Van Maren thinks that by simply implying the legality of killing certain babies rather than explicitly stating it, these laws aren't teaching injustice to the culture. But they are.

questions.question: What did Van Maren and Brahm conspicuously fail to address?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christians evaluating Van Maren and Brahm's arguments
questions.answer: A scriptural defense of pro-life incrementalism or a scriptural critique of abolitionism. Van Maren simply said he hadn't 'quite figured out their theology yet' and moved on, even though he has clearly studied the movement at length. Silberman finds this telling given that Free the States has published its biblical case publicly.
questions.quote: It is notable that Van Maren and Brahm did not attempt to make a scriptural defense of pro-life incrementalism or a scriptural critique of abolitionism.

related.title: The Antidote to Van Maren's Poison
related.reason: Direct sequel by Silberman responding to part 2 of the Brahm/Van Maren material.

related.title: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
related.reason: The earlier Wilberforce-historiography article that Silberman cites as part of the project provoking Van Maren's attack.

related.title: Are Nullification's Advocates the "Villains" of American History?
related.reason: Documents the 2019 nullification rebuttal Silberman references when describing Free the States stripping excuses from abolition bills.

related.title: Pro-Life Idaho State Rep: "We all know nullification doesn't happen in reality."
related.reason: Reinforces the 'anarchy' rebuttal by examining pro-life dismissals of state defiance of federal courts.

related.title: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
related.reason: Extends the critique of pro-life submission to SCOTUS that anchors point 8 of this article.

related.title: Four Problems With David Barton's Incrementalism Apologia
related.reason: Parallel rebuttal to a prominent pro-life voice defending incrementalism with similar Wilberforce/Garrison appeals.

related.title: Nullification
related.reason: Reference explainer for the constitutional doctrine underlying abolition bills' inseverability and state-defiance posture.
